---
title: "The Data Was Always There 4/7"
theme: "Irish Housing"
date: 2026-06-13
output:
  html:
    meta:
      css: ["@default", "@article", "@callout"]
      js: ["@copy-button", "@fold-details", "@callout", "@sidenotes", "@appendix"]
---

## 7. (Continued) — The Analytical Gap in the State Apparatus
 
### 7.7  Senior Civil Servants and the Approval of Unanalysed Claims
 
The lobby density documented in Section 7.2, and the professional services framing channel documented in Section 7.6, describe mechanisms by which the viability narrative reaches policymakers. This section addresses what happens when it arrives. The question isn’t only who is producing the analysis but whether the institutions that receive and act on it are applying sufficient analytical scrutiny to the claims being made. The evidence suggests they aren’t, and that this failure is structural rather than individual.
 
To be precise about what is being claimed and what isn’t: this section does not allege corruption, bad faith, or wilful negligence by any named individual within the civil service. Senior civil servants in the Departments of Finance and Housing, the Housing Finance Agency, the LDA, and An Bord Pleanála are, in the main, doing the work they are resourced, mandated, and trained to do. The problem this section documents is that the analytical question required to interrogate the viability narrative — what are Irish developer margins relative to EU peers on the same Eurostat statistical framework? — isn’t one that any of these institutions has, to this report's knowledge, formally posed or answered in any published policy document, Oireachtas submission, or internal review. The absence of the question isn’t the same as the presence of negligence. It may simply reflect the absence of the institutional capacity, mandate, and cross-dataset analytical framework required to ask it.
 
### 7.8  What Official Bodies Have and Have Not Found
 
The published record of official analytical work on housing sector profitability and viability is instructive precisely in what it contains and what it doesn’t.
 
**The Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG): ** In its 2022 Report on the Accounts of the Public Services, the C&AG examined the Housing Assistance Payment scheme and concluded that it 'doesn’t represent value for money' and 'doesn’t provide the State long-term assets.' It quantified the cumulative exchequer cost and noted the disproportionate flow of HAP payments to larger landlord portfolios. This is the closest any official body has come to a distributional analysis of housing subsidy flows, and it stopped at the question of state asset acquisition rather than proceeding to the question of developer margin extraction. The C&AG's finding was widely reported and widely ignored in subsequent policy design.
 
**The Parliamentary Budget Office (PBO): ** In its 2022 and 2023 analyses of Help-to-Buy, the PBO found that one-third of recipients didn’t need the scheme to meet their deposit requirement (deadweight), and that the scheme had a 'house price inflation effect.' These findings mirror exactly what this report's analysis predicts from first principles: demand-side subsidies flow to sellers, not buyers. The PBO explicitly cited the risk that Help-to-Buy supports developer margins rather than reducing housing costs. The scheme was extended regardless.
 
**The Central Bank of Ireland: ** In multiple Financial Stability Reviews and research papers, the Central Bank noted the house price inflation risk from demand-side interventions including Help-to-Buy, and flagged the concentration of the apartment development sector. None of these papers cross-referenced CNAPA01 developer margin data or Eurostat F41.1 GOS/GVA comparisons.
 
**The Housing Commission (2024): ** The Housing Commission's May 2024 report is the most thorough official analysis of the Irish housing system in a generation. It identified the 256,000-unit deficit, recommended a State-led delivery model, and analysed land costs as a primary driver. It didn’t calculate or reference developer GOS/GVA margins, didn’t compare Irish developer profitability against Eurostat EU peers, and didn’t quantify the planning-permission uplift using CSO land price data. All of these datasets were publicly available at the time of the Commission's deliberations.
 
**Budget documentation (2025–2026): ** The Department of Finance Budget 2026 documentation introducing the 9% VAT rate doesn’t contain a distributional analysis of who receives the VAT saving by developer size, concentration quintile, or subsidy stack. The impact assessment projects increased housing supply without modelling the developer margin effect. BDO, EY, Grant Thornton, and KPMG each published more detailed analyses of the measure than the Department of Finance's own budget documentation.
 
**The National Economic and Social Council (NESC):** The closest the official record comes to this report's diagnosis. NESC Council Report 145 (2018), Urban Development Land, Housing and Infrastructure: Fixing Ireland's Broken System, described the Irish housing system as 'speculative, volatile and expensive', called the urban land system dysfunctional, prepared a dedicated background paper on land value capture, and recommended active public land management on the Austrian, German and Dutch models. NESC Report 150 (2020) went further: it recommended establishing the LDA with compulsory acquisition, master-planning and land value capture powers, and called for the barriers to a Site Value Tax to be systematically addressed, noting that the Commission on Taxation had found a strong economic rationale for such a tax in 2009. NESC produced the structural diagnosis and the remedies. What it didn't produce (and wasn't resourced to produce) is the quantification: the sub-sector profitability data, the EU benchmarking, the measurement of who captures what. The diagnosis was published, the remedy was recommended, and the implementing departments adopted neither.
 
**The CSO's own international comparison (Business in Ireland 2020):** The most instructive entry, because it shows the analytical instrument calibrated not to see. The CSO's flagship enterprise publication, Business in Ireland 2020 (published November 2022), includes an EU27 comparison of construction profitability: Table 9.6, gross operating surplus as a percentage of turnover. Ireland's construction figure is 14.1% against an EU27 average of 10.7%. Above average, behind Malta, Hungary, Poland and Romania, and entirely unremarkable. The comparison is made at the whole-sector NACE F level, where the developer sub-sector's 81% value margin is diluted by the far larger builder and specialised-trades workforces, and on the GOS/turnover metric, which structurally understates margins in a sub-sector that books land value through high-turnover unit sales. The one official publication that did compare Irish construction profitability internationally did so at exactly the aggregation level, and on exactly the metric, at which the extraction documented in this report is invisible. This isn't a criticism of the CSO, whose publication serves its stated descriptive purpose. It's the precise illustration of why the Section 1 dual-source framework was necessary: the pattern only appears when the sector is disaggregated to three-digit NACE and measured against value added rather than turnover, a step no official analytical publication has taken.
 
The pattern across all seven is consistent: official bodies identify symptoms (HAP cost, Help-to-Buy deadweight, house price inflation, land cost) without diagnosing the structural cause (developer margin extraction at a rate structurally elevated above EU peers by a factor of 4.1× on a per-capita basis). They have partial vision — close enough to the problem to measure its effects, but not using the analytical framework required to identify its source.
 
> *Sources for this section: C&AG Report on the Accounts of the Public Services 2022. PBO publications 2022–2023 on Help-to-Buy. Central Bank Financial Stability Reviews and Notes. Housing Commission Report, May 2024. Department of Finance Budget 2026 documentation. NESC Council Reports 145 (2018) and 150 (2020); NESC Secretariat Paper No. 24 (2021) citing Commission on Taxation 2009. CSO, Business in Ireland 2020, International Comparisons chapter, Table 9.6 (published 2 November 2022): construction GOS/turnover, Ireland 14.1% vs EU27 10.7%.*
 
 
### 7.9  Why the Question Was Not Asked: Three Structural Explanations
 
#### Explanation 1 — The Mandate Gap
 
The CSO's mandate is measurement, not analysis. The C&AG's mandate is value-for-money audit of public expenditure, not economic analysis of private sector profitability. The Central Bank's mandate is financial stability, not distributional economics. The Housing Commission was constituted with a broad remit but a finite analytical staff. The Department of Finance is primarily a fiscal management institution. NESC, the body that came closest (producing the structural diagnosis in 2018 and the remedies in 2020), is an advisory council whose outputs bind nobody and whose analytical resources don't extend to distributional quantification. No institution within the Irish state apparatus has a specific mandate to conduct cross-dataset distributional analysis of the relationship between developer profitability, planning policy, and housing affordability. The Nevin Economic Research Institute and TASC have produced work in this space, but as civil society organisations without access to the administrative microdata held by Revenue and the CSO. No single institution failed here. What's missing is an institution with the mandate to bridge the analysis.
 
#### Explanation 2 — The Framing Environment
 
Policy analysis doesn’t begin from a neutral position. It begins from the questions that are considered worth asking, and those questions are shaped by the analytical frameworks that circulate in policy networks — through consultation processes, expert advisory groups, pre-Budget submissions, and the professional literature that civil servants read and cite. If the dominant analytical framework in that environment is the developer viability narrative, the viability gap is real, construction costs are high, margins are thin, supply requires support — then the question 'are Irish developer margins structurally elevated above EU peers?' doesn’t get asked, not because it is actively suppressed but because it doesn’t occur to those working within the framework as a question that needs answering. The Overton window of analytical consideration is itself shaped by the same epistemological environment that Section 7.6 documents in media coverage.
 
#### Explanation 3 — The Revolving Door
 
The Irish civil service, particularly at senior grades in the Departments of Finance and Housing and in the Housing Finance Agency, has a documented pattern of secondments, career transitions, and advisory relationships with professional services firms including those in the Big Four. This isn’t unique to Ireland; it is a feature of small economies with concentrated professional expertise, and it isn’t, in the form described here, improper. Senior civil servants who previously worked at KPMG, or who move to EY after their public service career, aren’t thereby compromised. But they bring with them, and carry forward, a shared conceptual vocabulary and a set of taken-for-granted analytical premises that shape which questions get asked and which frameworks get applied. When the dominant analytical vocabulary in both the civil service and the professional services sector shares the viability gap as a foundational premise, the absence of distributional counter-analysis isn’t surprising. It is the expected outcome of a shared intellectual environment.
 
None of these three explanations requires bad faith. Taken together, they describe a policy system that has been structurally incapable of asking the question this report asks, not because the data was unavailable, not because the individuals involved lacked intelligence or diligence, but because the institutional architecture, the framing environment, and the professional network through which analysis flows didn’t produce the cross-dataset distributional lens that the question requires. The CSO published CNAPA01 with six-decimal precision. The CSO published an EU27 construction profitability comparison in Business in Ireland 2020, at the whole-sector aggregation level where the sub-sector pattern is invisible. Eurostat published sbs_ovw_act with full country breakdowns. Eurostat published FATS data showing foreign-controlled construction in Ireland operating at twice the EU-average gross operating surplus margin. The CSO published ARA02 and RZLPA01. An Bord Pleanála published planning permission records. Glenveagh published its annual reports. The LDA announced its forward purchases. None of these data streams were connected by any official body into the analysis this report contains.
 
### 7.10  What Adequate Scrutiny Would Have Required
 
The analysis in this report was produced using only publicly available data sources, standard national accounts methodology, and cross-referencing against the same Eurostat Structural Business Statistics framework that Ireland's CSO submits to annually. It required no proprietary data, no Freedom of Information requests beyond those already cited in published journalism, and no analytical techniques beyond those taught in standard economics and public finance programmes. The GOS/GVA calculation is a ratio of two figures both published in CNAPA01. The per-capita comparison requires dividing Eurostat GOS figures by Eurostat population data.
 
What it required was the question. A senior civil servant in the Department of Finance, reviewing the Budget 2026 proposal for a 9% VAT rate on apartments, with adequate analytical support and a mandate to ask distributional questions, could have produced the following three-page analysis: what are Irish developer GOS/GVA margins from CNAPA01? How do they compare to EU peers from Eurostat sbs_ovw_act? What is the per-unit VAT saving, and how does it interact with the existing Croí Cónaithe and demand-side subsidy stack? That analysis would have produced the numbers in Section 8.3 of this report. It wasn’t produced. The Budget 2026 VAT measure was approved with an impact assessment that projected supply increases without modelling the distributional effect on developer margins, the same analytical omission that characterises every major housing subsidy approved in the preceding decade.
 
Bad policy is the lesser consequence of this analytical failure. The greater one is that the feedback mechanism that would otherwise correct policy doesn't function. When the C&AG finds HAP is poor value for money, when the PBO finds Help-to-Buy has deadweight costs, when the Central Bank flags demand-side inflation risks — these findings enter the policy record and are noted. But without the connecting analysis that shows why these symptoms persist (because the underlying developer extraction structure remains intact and is being actively supported), the response is always the same: a targeted improvement to the symptom, not a structural change to the cause. The Residential Zoned Land Tax is the clearest example: legislated to address land hoarding, lobbied into deferral before implementation, then partially superseded by forward-funding VAT relief that increases developer returns on exactly the transaction structure the RZLT was designed to disincentivise. Each of these policy moves was analytically defensible in isolation. None was cross-referenced against the full subsidy stack, the Eurostat margin comparison, or the distributional question of who benefits at the group level versus the project level.
 
This report does not claim to be comprehensive or definitive. It is the work of a private individual using public data and an AI analytical partner, completed without access to the administrative microdata or internal policy documents that state institutions hold. If this analysis is possible under those constraints, a fully resourced dedicated housing economics analytical unit, with access to Revenue's stamp duty microdata, CSO administrative datasets, and the Eurostat bilateral comparison framework — should be able to produce far more precise and granular findings. The absence of such a unit, and the absence of such analysis from the official record, is itself a policy choice, and one this report recommends reversing.
 
One specific statistical infrastructure gap is worth naming as a formal recommendation. The CSO publishes National Accounts by Institutional Sector (NA013), covering the savings and investment accounts of the corporate sector as a whole. This dataset shows all Irish companies' gross saving growing from €75 billion (2015) to €151 billion (2024) while building and construction capital formation grew from €14 billion to €36 billion, a useful macro picture. But NA013 doesn’t break out institutional sector accounts by NACE sub-sector. It is therefore impossible, from any publicly available CSO source, to determine what share of NACE F411’s gross operating surplus is retained in Ireland, distributed as dividends, or reinvested in productive assets — other than through the Eurostat SBS proxy of tangible asset investment rates, which this report uses and which shows 8% of GOS reinvested against an EU average of 62–89%. The CSO should publish NACE F41-specific institutional sector accounts, at minimum an abridged version showing the savings and investment accounts for the development and building construction sub-sectors. This would enable the state to verify (or refute) the central distributional finding of this report from a third independent national accounts framework. The data to construct it already exists in the CSO’s administrative systems. What doesn’t exist is the decision to publish it.
 
A second statistical-infrastructure gap deserves the same treatment. Eurostat’s Foreign Affiliates Statistics (dataset fats_activ) record the activity of foreign-controlled enterprises across the EU, and Ireland’s figures for foreign-controlled NACE F (Construction) are substantial. In 2022, 814 foreign-controlled construction enterprises operating in Ireland employed 15,701 people, generated €2,207 million in value added, €7,488 million in turnover, and €1,096 million in gross operating surplus. The GOS/value-added ratio works out at 49.7%, against an EU-27 average for foreign-controlled construction of 28.6%. That’s the same near-doubling of margin intensity that the Section 2 SBS analysis documents for Irish F411 against EU peers, now appearing in an entirely independent dataset built from a different statistical framework. Gross operating surplus per person employed runs at roughly €70,000 in Ireland against approximately €22,000 across the EU-27; value added per person at €141,000 against €76,000. The extraction signature shows up in the foreign-controlled segment of Irish construction at roughly twice the European intensity.
 
What the FATS data can’t do is sub-sectoral attribution. Foreign control is reported at the NACE F level only; the publicly available FATS doesn’t separate F411 (development of building projects) from F412 (construction of residential buildings) from F43 (specialised construction activities). So the €1.1 billion of foreign-controlled GOS can’t be precisely allocated between the developer sub-sector this report analyses, civil engineering, and the specialised construction segment, the latter being where multinational mechanical-and-electrical and fit-out contractors working on Irish data-centre, pharmaceutical, and semiconductor sites would sit. The foreign-controlled share of NACE F411 gross operating surplus specifically is therefore not publicly determinable. What can be said: the upper bound of foreign-controlled gross operating surplus in all of Irish construction is approximately 50% of the sector’s total; the margin intensity in that foreign-controlled segment exceeds the EU average for the equivalent segment by a factor of roughly two; and this is a third independent line of evidence for the extraction pattern that the SBS and CNAPA01 data document at the F411 level. Eurostat should publish FATS at NACE three-digit (F411, F412, F43) granularity, or member states should be required to compile and publish national-level foreign-affiliate statistics at that granularity, so the analytical question can actually be answered. As with NA013, the data to construct the breakdown exists in the underlying statistical returns. What doesn’t exist is the published series.
 
> *Sources: NA013 — CSO National Accounts by Institutional Sector. FATS — Eurostat fats_activ dataset, foreign-controlled enterprises in NACE F (Construction), Ireland and EU-27, 2021–2023, downloaded 10 June 2026. EU-27 averages calculated  from country-level FATS data; sub-sector unavailability confirmed from dataset structure.*
